If you want to grow your X (formerly Twitter) account you need to:
\- tweet a lot. Like more than you think. If you think you’re tweeting a lot, tweet more. The timeline moves fast.
\- treat as your notes app. That’s the advice I received when I first started and it has worked wonders. Everything you’d typically write in your notes now goes here. The more unhinged the better. Leave nothing unsaid. Leave no stone unturned.
\- practice writing and hone in on your unique voice. It’s like yelling into the void for awhile bc no one follows you. Good. Keep going.
\- share photos of yourself and your life. People want to know you’re a real person.
\- have an opinion. It’s okay if it’s different. But also!!! Be open to changing your mind. This app can get super negative super fast but people used to be capable of having conversations with people of opposing opinions. Practice!! Also. Your opinion is not a fact.
\- I don’t think you need a “niche” but you have 2-5 things you focus on. For me: fashion, running, relationships, life musings
\- reply to people and find people you enjoy following!! Build a community. It takes time and effort but it’s worth it.
\- have fun. It’s not that serious. Remember it’s not real. If it all goes away tomorrow, your real life continues.
\- you’ll always remember the first time you got bullied and dragged through the pits of twitter hell. Congrats. You made it.

posts.text:
I sat down with [@Jacob\_\_Siegel](https://nitter.net/Jacob__Siegel "Jacob Siegel") to ask him what his new book can teach us about AI, progressivism, Palantir, the future of writing, and why Tablet has been on such a tear lately.
A few quick notes on what stood out to me.
He thinks the US government has built something monstrous, which looks all-powerful but is really very feeble.
He traces it back to Vietnam, where the US first invested seriously in surveillance and algorithmic data systems to combat insurgent behavior.
But the US lost in Vietnam!
This problem runs all the way up to Russiagate, which was both unprecedented narrative coordination and a comedy of errors.
On Palantir
Siegel used Palantir firsthand as an Army intelligence officer in Afghanistan and found it superior to the government systems but there was a new problem.
The graphics were so compelling that analysts would get mesmerized but not really develop a better understanding.
Palantir created a kind of digital dependency and quasi-mystical feeling that the truth of the war was about to reveal itself if you just looked hard enough into the computer.
Why progressivism is more inclined to information control
He doesn't put much stock in the current alignments between AI companies and the Trump administration. Anthropic clashing with Hegseth, etc.—he sees this as volatile and highly contingent.
Siegel says progressivism is intrinsically more linked to information control—socialism, cybernetics in the Soviet Union, rational central planning, etc.—because it vests authority in an expert administrative class whose power derives specifically from consuming and analyzing information.
Second, progressivism views society as a holistic organism or machine with an informational unity that can be accessed and optimized by pulling enough data.
On AI and journalism
Siegel expects entire categories of journalism to become fully AI-produced, expanding over time before hitting a limit and possibly contracting. What remains human will be investigative work connected to secrets, human networks of power, social relationships.
What Tablet is doing right
I mentioned that I thought Tablet has been on a notable run in recent years, publishing quite a lot of good stuff I've actually taken the time to read. What's the secret sauce, I asked.
He said that as a Jewish magazine it's written by people who see themselves as outsiders, unwilling to compromise their identity, but also having an obligation to the world. Editor Alana Newhouse has resisted pressure to universalize it, believing that the specificity is what makes it special.
The other key ingredient is simply giving talented people room to run—writers are allowed to pursue serious work for long periods of time. He himself spent nearly three years on the essay that became the book, waiting until the Twitter Files gave him the right hook.
What's coming next
Siegel thinks a Democrat—possibly Newsom—likely wins in 2028, and the scenario that worries him is that information control gets more competent.
Biden's visible physical decomposition combined with an overreaching information apparatus and political manias (COVID protocols, Russiagate, the Kamala swap) was so brazen and absurd that it was self-defeating and helped Trump win.
"5% more competence and I think they'd still be in power."
Conversation below.

I took my time last night to study the Algorithm released by Elon's X, and one big motivation that prompted me was understanding how the new algorithm works so that I and my followers can get the maximum benefit from it.
Here is what I found out:
DOs: For Maximum Reach
1\. Reply bait is real. Posts that make people respond, argue, or agree loudly get pushed hardest. Write opinions, not announcements.
2\. Reply to every comment on your post. An active thread signals sustained engagement and keeps your post alive in the scoring window.
3\. Post when your audience is online. The algorithm trims old posts from memory. Early engagement in the first 60 minutes determines if non-followers ever see your post.
4\. Reposts and quote tweets beat likes. A like is a light signal. A repost drops your content into someone else's feed and starts a chain reaction.
5\. Stop scrolling and engage on big posts. Leaving a strong reply on a viral post exposes your account to audiences already interested in that topic.
6\. Write posts people read to the end. Dwell time is tracked. A weak opening line kills your score before anyone gets to the substance.
DON'Ts: What Kills Your Reach
1\. Do not post back to back. The algorithm deliberately suppresses repeated authors in the same feed session. Space your posts out.
2\. Do not post and go silent. An abandoned thread with no follow-up responses is a dead signal.
3\. Do not rely on hashtags. They are not part of the scoring formula at all.
4\. Do not post content that makes people hit Not Interested, Mute or Block. Those carry negative weights that actively push your posts down.
5\. Do not obsess over likes. The algorithm weighs replies and reposts far more heavily.
The formula is simple: make people stop and view your post, respond, and share. Everything else follows.

Click Get API Key → Create API Key.
Name it specifically for this use case — I name mine per brand or per project (e.g. “Imagen Test - \[Brand\]”).
Reason: API keys can leak, and if one does, you want to know exactly which workflow it belonged to so you can revoke it without taking everything else down.
Copy the key. Don’t paste it anywhere shared.
Tell Claude to build the skill.
In a fresh Claude chat, give it the skill creator (or tell Claude to build a reusable skill that takes image prompts as input and outputs generated images via the API).
Paste your API key. Tell Claude:
Set up the skill. Test it on a basic prompt
Claude will:
1.Look up available image gen models on Google AI Studio
2.Find Nano Banana 2 (Google’s current top image model — the one you actually want)
3.Write the skill code wrapping the API call
4\. Run the test prompt against the API
5.Output the test image inline
If Google Drive is connected, Claude saves the output there too. If Co-work is set up to write to local files, the image lands directly in your computer’s file system.
Step 3 — Save as a Claude skill.
Once the test works, tell Claude: “Save this as a skill named \[whatever\].” I name mine “Imagen Gen Skill - \[Brand\]” so they’re scoped per project.
Now the skill is in your library. Available across all your chats, on desktop AND browser. Generate images by name-dropping the skill in any future chat.

posts.text:
I Created YouTube Channel After YouTube Channel… and Got Absolutely ZERO Views Every Single Time.
No impressions. No clicks. Complete silence.
If that sounds familiar, you're not alone. And I know exactly what questions are racing through your mind right now because I asked myself the same ones:
"Is my computer flagged by YouTube?"
"Do I need to buy a new laptop?"
"Should I be using proxies or antidetect browsers?"
"Am I shadow-banned forever?"
Let me walk you through what's actually happening—and more importantly, how to fix it.
Q: Is there still hope, or is my setup permanently ruined?
A: There is 100% hope. Here's the truth: if you can still upload videos, your account isn't banned.
What you're experiencing is what I call a "low trust phase." Think of it like this: YouTube doesn't know you yet. It doesn't trust that your content is worth showing to people. So it's basically putting you in a "waiting room" until you prove you're legitimate.
The good news? This can absolutely be fixed. You're not blocked. You're just not trusted yet.
Q: Why are my videos getting zero impressions?
A: This isn't about your thumbnail, title, or even your content quality. Zero impressions means YouTube isn't even testing your video with real viewers.
Think of YouTube's algorithm like a careful investor. Before it "invests" (shows your video to people), it wants to see proof that you're worth the risk.
Here's what triggers this low-trust state:
Creating multiple new channels in a short time – This looks like spam behavior to YouTube
Deleting and reuploading the same videos repeatedly – This signals panic and inexperience
Accounts that seem automated or fake – No watch history, no engagement, just uploading
Unusual patterns – Like brand new accounts immediately uploading daily
YouTube's system isn't punishing you on purpose. It's just being cautious because these patterns match what spammers and bots do.
Q: Is my computer or IP address the problem?
A: Your device is only a tiny piece of the puzzle.
Yes, YouTube can see some device information. But here's what matters way more: your behavior.
YouTube cares about:
How you use the account
Whether you act like a real person
If your content gets genuine engagement
Your uploading patterns and consistency
Your laptop itself? Not the main issue.
Q: So should I buy a new computer to start fresh?
A: No. Save your money.
I know it's tempting to think a clean slate will solve everything, but here's the reality: If you repeat the same behaviors on a new computer, you'll get the same results.
It's like moving to a new city but bringing all your old habits with you. The location changed, but you didn't.
The problem isn't the hardware. It's the approach.
Q: Okay, so what's ACTUALLY going wrong then?
A: You're stuck in a restart loop.
Here's what's happening:
You create a channel → It gets zero views
You panic and think "This channel is dead"
You delete everything or create a new channel
You start over at zero trust again
Repeat
Every time you restart, you're pressing the reset button on trust. You never give YouTube's algorithm enough time to learn about your content, test it with viewers, or build any momentum.
It's like planting a seed, digging it up after two days because nothing grew, planting a new seed somewhere else, and wondering why you never get a garden.
You're not giving anything time to grow.
Q: So what should I actually do instead?
Here's your step-by-step recovery plan:
Step 1: Pick ONE channel and commit to it
Stop creating new channels. Stop deleting content. Choose one and stick with it for at least 30-60 days.
Step 2: Act like a real human on YouTube
Before you upload anything else, spend 3-5 days using YouTube normally:
Watch videos in your niche (10-15 minutes per day)
Leave genuine comments on videos you actually enjoy
Like videos and subscribe to channels
Search for topics related to what you want to create
This signals to YouTube: "This is a real person, not a bot."
Step 3: Upload like a real creator, not a desperate one
Do:
Upload one video and leave it up (no matter what happens)
Choose simple, clear topics that answer specific questions
Focus on searchable content first (tutorials, how-tos, explanations)
Write a detailed description and use relevant tags
Don't:
Upload multiple videos in one day
Delete and reupload the same content
Make drastic changes to your channel daily
Obsess over analytics in the first 48 hours
Step 4: Share your video outside of YouTube
YouTube's algorithm pays attention to where your traffic comes from. A few real viewers help build initial trust.
Share your video:
On relevant Reddit communities (helpfully, not spammy)
In niche Facebook groups
On Twitter with useful context
In Discord servers related to your topic
With friends or family who might genuinely be interested
Even 10-20 real views from external sources tells YouTube: "Real people are choosing to watch this."
Step 5: Upload consistently but slowly
Don't rush. Give YouTube time to test each video.
Recommended schedule:
Week 1: Upload 1 video, wait 3-4 days
Week 2: Upload 1 video, wait 3-4 days
Week 3-4: Upload 1-2 videos per week
This gives the algorithm time to:
Test your content with small audiences
Gather data on watch time and engagement
Decide if your content is worth promoting
Q: What does actual recovery look like?
Recovery is gradual, not instant. Here's what progress actually looks like:
Video 1: 5-20 impressions (YouTube is barely testing)
Video 2: 30-80 impressions (slight increase, still cautious)
Video 3: 100-300 impressions (algorithm is gaining confidence)
Video 4: 500+ impressions (you're building trust)
This is normal. This is healthy growth from a low-trust state.
You're not looking for viral success right away. You're looking for steady increases in impressions, which prove YouTube is starting to trust your channel.
Q: How long does this take?
Honest answer: 3-8 weeks of consistent, patient effort.
I know that's not the "get rich quick" answer you might want to hear. But this is the realistic timeline for going from zero impressions to a channel that's actually being tested by YouTube's algorithm.
The creators who succeed are the ones who outlast the low-trust phase instead of restarting every time it gets hard.
What's the Real Lesson Here?
Stop trying to outsmart YouTube's system.
Start trying to build trust with it.
YouTube's algorithm isn't your enemy. It's just cautious. It's designed to filter out spam and promote real creators who make valuable content.
Your job isn't to trick it. Your job is to convince it—through consistent, patient, genuine behavior—that you're the real deal.
Your Action Plan (Starting Today):
✅ Stop creating new channels – Commit to one
✅ Use YouTube like a normal person – Watch, comment, engage for a few days
✅ Upload one simple, searchable video – Don't overthink it
✅ Share it with real people – Get those first genuine views
✅ Wait 3-4 days – Give YouTube time to test it
✅ Repeat slowly and consistently – Build momentum over weeks, not days
You're not shadow-banned. You're not cursed. You're just in the trust-building phase.

We rebuilt our entire content engine with Claude Code.
20+ people on our team posting. $151K MRR added in 87 days.
It started as a random experiment with 1, then 2, then 3 people posting. Then we built it into a replicable system.
Here's the architecture (27 skills, 6 layers):
1/ FOUNDATION (built once per person)
→ Voice profile: 25-question conversation, not a form. How someone talks before AI touches anything.
→ ICP document: 3 buyer tiers, mapped to the language each tier uses to search for solutions.
→ Content pillars: 3 to 5 themes every post traces back to.
Many people skip this and go straight to writing. Then wonder why everything sounds the same.
2/ RESEARCH LAYER (runs weekly)
→ Apify browses LinkedIn for what's performing in your niche right now
→ Reddit for real audience pain language
→ YouTube for frameworks worth adapting
→ X/Twitter for live debates
→ Fireflies transcripts from client calls (the highest-signal source)
Six workers in parallel.
3/ PRODUCTION LINE
→ Hook generator: 50 templates, 20+ variations per idea
→ Copy developer: full draft in your documented voice
→ Post grader: 5-dimension rubric, /50. Below 38? Back to rewrite.
→ Gemini for visuals. Figma + Canva for refinement.
AI drafts. You sharpen. The voice profile is the guardrail.
4/ REPURPOSE (one post, many formats)
→ One LinkedIn post that performed well = one validated idea
→ Restructured into X/Twitter thread, newsletter, blog, video script, carousel
→ Each format rebuilt for how people consume on that platform
Repurposing is not copy-pasting. It is restructuring.
5/ REFRESH + MAINTAIN
→ Feedback capture logs every session automatically
→ Pattern recognition every 5 sessions: what keeps getting rewritten, where voice drifts
→ Monthly voice refresh: pull new transcripts, compare against voice profile
→ Content audit quarterly: are your pillars still relevant to where the market moved?
The system gets smarter the longer it runs.
6/ DELIVERY
→ ClickUp for client handoff
→ Taplio for scheduling
→ Performance data fed back into Claude for the next cycle
One terminal. 27 skills. Each does one job.
Research → ideas → hooks → copy → grade → repurpose → refresh → deliver.
This is how 24 people sound like themselves, at scale.

Foundation
brand-voice: Loads my full voice profile, ICP, and content examples so anything I write sounds like me, not generic AI; built so every other skill inherits the same voice instead of re-explaining it each time.
positioning-artifact-builder: Turns Voice-of-Customer into a documented ICP, JTBD, and ownable idea; built to be the upstream source of truth so all downstream content says the same sharp thing.

LinkedIn

linkedin-hook-writer: Generates scroll-stopping opening lines from 7 proven formulas across 880 analyzed posts; built because the first line decides whether the post gets read at all.
X / Twitter
x-article-creation: Writes a 1,500–2,000 word tactical X article with intro and lead-magnet CTA; built because X long-form is now a primary growth engine.
qt-writer: Writes quote-tweet copy trained on my top 186 QTs ranked by bookmarks; built because the QT format drives saves and needed its own voice model.
qt-orchestrator: Pairs infographic briefs with matching quote-tweet copy in one pass; built so the visual and the words ship together instead of being two disconnected steps.
infographic-brief: Generates 3–5 bookmark-worthy GPT Image 2 infographic briefs from a transcript or article; built to turn one piece of long-form into a visual content pack.
gpt-image2-prompter: Produces on-brand GPT Image 2 prompts for infographics and lifestyle shots; built so image generation stays on-brand without manual prompt engineering.
Podcast
podcast-content-pack: Generates show notes, intro script, and YouTube description in one parallel pass and files them to Drive; built to collapse post-production into a single command.
podcast-show-notes: Writes full show notes in my exact format from a transcript; built so every episode ships with consistent supporting copy.
youtube-intro: Writes high-retention 30–50 second intros from a proven outlier formula; built because the first 40 seconds decide retention.
youtube-description: Writes the full structured description box from a transcript; built so the description is never the bottleneck on publishing.
youtube-title-outliers: Scrapes real outlier videos via vidIQ and generates 5 copycat title variations; built to base titles on what's actually overperforming, not guesses.
thumbnail-brief: Generates 5 split-testable thumbnail briefs from a reference image and title; built so thumbnails are a testable system, not a one-off design.
podcast-guest-pitch: Researches a specific episode and writes a personalized guest pitch; built to make the guesting pipeline scalable.
podcast-research: Finds podcasts matching outreach criteria and adds them to Notion; built to feed the guesting pipeline with qualified targets.
Newsletter
ai-operator-brief: Writes the Kit-ready AI Operator Brief for the Return My Time list; built so the weekly brief ships in editorial voice with zero reformatting.
coreys-notes-newsletter: Turns a transcript or brain dump into a Kit-ready Corey's Notes newsletter with subject lines; built to repurpose existing audio into list growth.
ai-weekly-intel: Researches and drafts the weekly AI intel brief for the Skool community; built to keep the community fed with no-fluff value on a schedule.
Copy / Conversion
direct-response-copy: Writes landing pages, emails, sales copy, and CTAs that convert; built because persuasive copy is a different muscle than educational content.
lead-magnet: Generates lead magnet concepts with hooks and a bridge to the paid offer; built so top-of-funnel ideas connect to revenue, not just list size.
returnmytime-blog: Writes SEO-optimized blog posts for Return My Time; built to turn the blog into a passive discovery channel.

posts.text:
Maybe a lot of you already know this, but for those who don't: running a fanmade account is not easy.
Even if you have a life outside of Twitter, the accounts you see with thousands of followers are the result of being here practically 24/7, looking for content, writing tweets that hook people, doing real time translations, finding fancams in seconds so nobody misses anything.
So yeah, when someone asks "why aren't there accounts for Sangwon?"... there are. The difference is the support. Giving them interactions, sharing their content, keeping them active. That's what makes an account grow.
Those of us behind these pages have sacrificed real things. In my case sleep, there are days I go to bed at 7am just so you guys have the info as fast as possible. I'm not exaggerating, being a fan feels like an actual job even though you don't get paid.
Anyone can open a fanmade account but keeping it consistent is a whole other thing, and that's what sets the big accounts apart.
Hopefully more of you start appreciating that effort 🖤 I'm glad Sangwon and Leo have accounts big enough to promote them because not every fandom has that luxury.

Does Elon Musk deserve to be the world's first trillionaire?
Elon doesn’t just lead companies, he founds and scales transformative technologies that reshape civilization.
Here’s a clear breakdown of his roles and key achievements:
⚡ TESLA, INC.
Leads product design, engineering, manufacturing strategy, and global operations for EVs, battery technology, and energy products. Sets the vision for Full Self-Driving, Optimus humanoid robots, and next-gen vehicles. Transformed Tesla from near-bankruptcy into the world’s leading EV maker and the first U.S. automaker to reach a $1 trillion market cap. Launched the vehicles that made premium EVs mainstream: Roadster, Model S, X, 3, Y, Cybertruck, and Semi. Built global Gigafactories, pioneered large-scale battery production and energy storage, and is advancing autonomy and robotics.
🚀 SPACEX (including Starlink)
Founder, CEO & Chief Engineer. Leads design of all rockets (Falcon family, Starship) and spacecraft (Dragon). Directs reusable launch systems, crewed spaceflight, Mars colonization, and the Starlink satellite constellation for global broadband. Revolutionized spaceflight with reusable rockets and first private crewed ISS missions. Deployed thousands of Starlinks to connect underserved regions. Major NASA contractor.
𝕏, X Corp
Sets the vision for 𝕏 as the “everything app” and real-time information platform with free speech as a core principle. Oversees tech, product, algorithms, monetization, and Grok AI integration. Acquired Twitter for $44 billion in 2022, rebranded to 𝕏, implemented major efficiencies, shifted to subscription/creator models, and reduced heavy content moderation.
🤖 xAI
Leads development of advanced AI systems to understand the true nature of the universe. Oversees Grok model development, the Colossus supercomputing cluster, and massive compute scaling. Rapidly built frontier AI capabilities as a truth-seeking alternative and is integrating with SpaceX for space-based compute and X for real-time data.
🧠 NEURALINK
Directs R&D for high-bandwidth brain-machine interface implants. Oversees hardware, software, surgical robotics, and clinical strategy. Achieved the first successful human brain-chip implants starting in 2024, enabling thought-controlled operation. Expanded trials by 2026 with plans for high-volume automated production and procedures to help paralysis patients and advance human-AI symbiosis.
🚇 THE BORING COMPANY
Leads development of faster, lower-cost tunneling technology and underground electric transport. Built and operates the Las Vegas Convention Center Loop, a functional public underground transit system, while demonstrating significantly cheaper and quicker tunneling methods.
Through vision, technical leadership, relentless execution, and willingness to take enormous risks across electric vehicles, space exploration, AI, brain interfaces, social media, and infrastructure, Elon has created massive value for humanity and investors alike.
He deserves every damn penny and then some.

how to grow your twitter 101
my older account had 10k followers and this one’s grown pretty quickly to 3k followers and some tweets have gotten some crazy reach
lemme try to break it down
1/ understand the algorithm
you see what you like so the algorithm tries to show you posts from people that you like or post in the domains that you like/follow
if you like ai, you’ll see a lot of ai posts
so decide what your niche or set of niches are going to be
2/ post a lot and be consistent
your account health improves + engagement increases
don’t worry about what people will think, you’re not as important - don’t overthink, just post a lot
3/ be raw and non-generic
don’t post generic stuff, make it raw and add a personal touch
4/ engage with people
but be original and avoid ai slop, it shows when you use ai slop
5/ build a plan and then let it become a habit
once you get a feel of this, you’ll become a content wh and it’ll naturally come to you
you’ll recognise trends, what works and more
6/ don’t be serious, no one cares
let your heart out and have fun

Real research:
→ Read 200+ customer reviews and steal the exact phrases they use
→ Watch winning ads and write down WHY each hook actually works
→ Study TikTok comments until you can literally predict objections before they happen
→ Actually sit with the research until ad ideas start writing themselves in your head
You'll know your research worked when you can close the Google Doc and still write 3 killer hooks from memory.
That's the difference between collecting data and actually understanding your customer.

I don't think I'm special.
I'm not lucky either.
Yes, I've made over $300K from freelancing. Over $150,000 of that on Upwork alone.
Yes, I'm a Top Rated Plus freelancer. Top 1%.
Yes, I have clients that have paid me $20k, $40k, and even $60k for work I did from my bedroom.
And I still don't think I'm very different from you.
Matter of fact, I've coached students who were smarter than me.
Better skilled. More experienced. Some of them had degrees I didn’t have.
They just didn't know the right things yet.
So how have I been able to do all of this?
One word: knowledge.
In 2023, I made peace with an uncomfortable statement.
“Something you don't know is the reason you're still where you are.”
Nobody is 10x smarter than you.
But there are people making 10x, even 100x what you make right now.
And the gap isn't talent. It's information.
If you knew what they knew, you'd get what they get.
This is why I spent hundreds of hours building The Proposal Templates Bible.
I went over every single thing that has made me money on Upwork.
Every hook that worked. Every framework I tested. Every pattern I noticed in the proposals that won vs. the ones that got ignored.
And I put all of it into one ebook.
Here's what you get:
👉🏾 The CLOSE Framework. A 5-part persuasion sequence that turns a skimmable proposal into one clients can’t ignore.
👉🏾 10 Hook Types with 10 examples each. Each one explained with examples across different niches.
👉🏾 100+ done-for-you proposal templates across 14+ skill categories and 40+ niches. Content writing, web dev, design, digital marketing, video editing, VA, mobile dev, data, AI services, and more.
👉🏾 A Hook Selection Quick Guide that tells you exactly which hook to use for which type of job post.
Bonus:
👉🏾 CLOSE Beyond Upwork. How to adapt the framework for cold email, LinkedIn DMs, Twitter/X, agency contact forms, and referrals.
How much is it? Just N6,500.
My 1-on-1 session and mentorship is way more than that.
Not everyone can afford it comfortably.
That is why I am selling it for N6500.
This is just my tiny way of giving back to the community.
If you want to finally write proposals that get read, land clients that pay well, and stop guessing every time you apply for a job, this is for you.
I promise you, you won't find anything this complete at this price.
Link to buy is in the comments.
Price goes to ₦10,000 after Sunday. After that, it stays there, only going up.
If you want it, now's the time.

posts.text:
x gave every person on earth free access to 600 million monthly users. free distribution. free reach to strangers. no application. no approval. no ad spend required. the single most valuable distribution channel in 2026 and you opened it this morning to scroll.
here's what happens when you use it differently. every piece. profile to product to payment. nothing withheld.
the profile.
your bio is a storefront, not a gravestone. "founder. builder. dreamer." converts nobody. it needs three lines: what you do, who you do it for, and what happens when they click the link. "i help faceless accounts turn tweets into $5K months. free blueprint below." that's a storefront. profile picture is a brand logo. no face required. no name required. your pinned tweet is your single best CTA. your bio link goes to ONE thing. not a linktree with 6 options, a podcast nobody asked for, and a calendly with zero bookings.
the content.
3 tweets per day. never more. the algorithm applies exponential decay after 3, so tweet 4 actively buries tweet 3. you write all 3 in 15 minutes using claude with a voice primer that makes every output sound like a human who's been in the niche for 3 years. 2 value tweets that teach, reveal, or tear something down. 1 CTA tweet that drives traffic to your free guide through a keyword comment. that ratio. every day.
the value tweets aren't opinions. they hand over a specific copyable asset: a scoreboard, a step sequence, a tool list, a teardown of how something works. the kind of tweet people screenshot and send to their friends. that's what earns bookmarks (10x algorithmic weight) and reposts (20x weight). opinions get likes (1x weight). assets get bookmarked and shared.
the CTA tweet.
80% pure value. a mechanism, a teardown, real numbers. strong enough that someone saves it even if they never read the ask. then: "i wrote the whole thing out. 11 pages. comment BACKEND, repost, and follow. i'll send it free."
every comment fires 13.5x reply weight. every repost fires 20x. then you sit in the replies for 60 minutes answering every commenter. each reply chain fires 75x. that's the heaviest positive signal in the entire x algorithm. one CTA tweet engineered this way produces more algorithmic weight than 50 regular posts. this is what separates a CTA getting 200 views from one getting 200K.
what to sell.
whatever you already know that someone 2 years behind you would pay for the shortcut on. "how to land your first 3 freelance clients through cold DMs." "the system for getting your first 1,000 followers on x." "how to start and scale a faceless account from scratch." you aren't inventing knowledge. you're packaging what's already in your head.
8-15 pages. one problem. one solution. one afternoon with claude. upload to gumroad. done. the product costs $0 to make, $0 to store, $0 to deliver. the 10,000th copy costs exactly as much as the first: nothing. 90% margin after gumroad's 10% cut.
the pricing.
$9-17: buyers assume it's garbage. price signals low effort. they scroll past.
$27-67: "probably worth something." they consider it.
$67-197: "this person is serious." they buy and implement.
$497+: pre-sold. they read the headline, check one proof screenshot, and pay. refund rate near zero because they were already committed before they clicked.
start at $67 minimum. raise it every time new proof enters the community. the $67 buyer asks fewer questions and refunds less often than the $19 buyer. every single time. underpricing isn't humility. it's the most expensive mistake in info selling.
where to send them.
not your product page. not a linktree. your free community.
when someone comments the keyword, tweethunter auto-DMs them two links: your free telegram community and your product page. they join the community first. inside they see other members posting wins, screenshots, questions, progress. they read the free guide pinned at the top. the guide delivers genuine value AND names the exact problem your paid product solves.
by the time they finish reading, the community has already done the selling. nobody pitched them. the room did. the people around them did. the proof on the walls did.
never send the free guide in the DM. send them to the community. let the environment deliver it. this one change is the difference between a community that converts at 1% and one that converts at 4-6%.
the price ladder.
free guide at $0 → captures and warms.
front-end product at $67-$100 → gets the first transaction.
mid-ticket system at $497 → where real revenue lives.
high-ticket partnership at $5K → where profit lives.
same audience. ascending commitment. the person who grabbed the free guide last month is the same person buying the $497 system today. and the person who implemented the $497 system is the one applying for the $5K partnership next month. you don't need new people at every level. you need one door and a hallway.
the math.
one CTA tweet reaches 100K views. 0.5% comment the keyword. 500 people enter the community. 4% convert to the $67 product in 30 days. 20 sales. $1,206.
that's one tweet. you post one CTA per day. the community doesn't reset between tweets. it stacks. by month 3: 2,800 members. price raised to $97. second product launched at $497. 1% of the community buys it. 28 sales at $497 = $12,516. plus the $97 product underneath. combined: $15,000+ months.
by month 6 the compounding is undeniable. community at 8,400. proof everywhere. conversion rate climbing because every new member sees more results than the last one. the math stops being theoretical and starts being a bank statement.
the whole infrastructure:
claude: $20/month
gumroad: $0
telegram: $0
tweethunter: $49/month
total: $69/month
$69 in tools. 90% margins. no warehouse. no employees. no investors. no permission.
every traditional business on earth accepted 3-30% margins because it had no choice. supply chains. storefronts. inventory risk. employee overhead. shipping.
you're sitting on a platform that distributes your content to 600 million people for free, selling a product with zero production cost and 90% margins, building a community that does the selling for you while you sleep.
this is the most asymmetric business opportunity in 2026. it's not even close. the infrastructure costs less than a spotify subscription and the upside is uncapped.
i wrote the entire backend system down. 11 pages. how the profile converts. how the free guide captures. how the community warms. how one offer at one price closes people who were already ready. how the price ladder compounds. every piece of the system behind this account and every account i've helped build. it's free.
comment BACKEND, repost this, and follow. i'll send it.

posts.text:
Growing your account on Twitter requires building and engaging a real audience. Here are some key ways to grow your account:
1\. Post Consistent and Valuable Content
Share tweets regularly that people find interesting, informative, or entertaining. Consistency keeps your audience active and helps the algorithm show your content to more people.
2\. Engage With Your Audience
Reply to comments, like replies, and interact with people who engage with your tweets. When followers feel noticed and appreciated, they are more likely to stay active and support your content.
3\. Use Trending Topics and Hashtags
Posting about trending issues or using relevant hashtags can help your tweets reach a wider audience beyond your followers.
4\. Create Relatable and Shareable Content
Tweets that people relate to or learn from are more likely to be retweeted and shared, which exposes your account to new audiences.
5\. Engage With Other Creators
Reply to tweets from bigger accounts and join conversations in your niche. When your replies are insightful or funny, people may check your profile and follow you.
6\. Build a Clear Niche
Focus on a specific area like campus life, politics, entertainment, or motivation. When people know what type of content to expect, they are more likely to follow and stay engaged.
7\. Avoid Engagement Farming
Focus on genuine interactions instead of forcing likes or comments. Authentic engagement builds a loyal audience and helps long-term growth.

BREAKING: If you're not using Claude at your job, you're already behind.
Copy these 11 prompts:1. Multi-File Project Auditor
Paste to Claude: "Load my entire project folder: \[upload files\]. Analyze: architectural decisions that should change, technical debt to tackle first, security vulnerabilities, performance bottlenecks, and outdated dependencies. Give me a prioritized action plan."2. Context Memory Vault
Paste to Claude: "I'm working on \[project name\]. Here's everything you need to retain: \[paste project context\]. From now on, reference this context in every response without me repeating it. Summarize what you'll remember."3. Parallel Work Manager
Paste to Claude: "I need to complete these tasks simultaneously: \[list tasks\]. Spawn agent teams: assign each task to a separate agent, coordinate between them, report progress from all agents, merge results when done. Run them in parallel."4. Adaptive Problem Navigator
Paste to Claude: "Complex problem: \[describe problem\]. Don't answer immediately. First, assess: how deep should you think about this (quick/medium/deep reasoning), what information is missing, what assumptions to challenge. Then solve it at the appropriate depth."5. Mistake Spotter
Paste to Claude: "Review everything I've done so far in this conversation. Find: logical errors I made, assumptions that might be wrong, places where I'm overconfident, risks I haven't considered. Challenge my thinking before I commit to this approach."6. Long-Term Project Assistant
Paste to Claude: "This is a month-long project: \[describe project\]. Track decisions from past conversations, context from weeks ago, shifting requirements, and what we've already tried. Use context compaction so you never forget anything important as we continue."7. Cross-Repo Connector
Paste to Claude: "I have two projects: \[project A\] and \[project B\]. Load both codebases. Find how they should integrate, which API endpoints to create, shared data structures needed, the migration strategy, and what breaks if we connect them. Map the full integration."8. Decision Simulator
Paste to Claude: "I'm deciding between these options: \[option A vs option B\]. Simulate the next 6 months for each path. Compare risks, hidden costs, opportunity upside, second order effects, and likely failure points. Recommend the highest leverage choice."9. Content Repurposing Engine
Paste to Claude: "Turn this single piece of content: \[paste content\] into a LinkedIn post, Twitter thread, newsletter, YouTube script, carousel outline, and short form video hook set. Keep the tone native to each platform."10. Meeting Intelligence Assistant
Paste to Claude: "Here are my meeting notes/transcript: \[paste notes\]. Extract key decisions, unresolved blockers, action items, deadlines, ownership, and hidden concerns people hinted at but didn’t directly say."11. Reverse Engineer Anything
Paste to Claude: "Here is an example I admire: \[paste example\]. Reverse engineer the strategy behind it. Break down structure, psychology, hooks, distribution method, monetization angle, and why it works."12. Skill Accelerator
Paste to Claude: "I want to become highly skilled at \[skill\]. Create a 30 day learning roadmap with daily exercises, practical projects, feedback loops, mistakes to avoid, and ways to measure improvement."Bonus: AI Workflow Architect
Paste to Claude: "Design an AI powered workflow for my job as a \[role\]. Include tools, prompts, automations, delegation systems, quality checks, and how to reduce manual work by at least 50%."
